title: William—An Englishman
description: William—An Englishman — knowledge fragments for AI agents

memes.label: "Balham"
memes.description: "A residential district of south London where Griselda's aunt lived, and where William and Griselda's wedding took place in July 1914."

memes.label: "Battersea Park"
memes.description: "A public park in London where William Tully and Griselda dedicated their lives to the service of Progress and Humanity shortly before their wedding in July 1914."

memes.label: "Belgian Ardennes"
memes.description: "A secluded forested region of Belgium where William Tully and Griselda spent their honeymoon in the summer of 1914, unknowingly at the outbreak of the First World War."

memes.label: "Belgium"
memes.description: "The wartime setting where the novel's central drama unfolds — Madame Amberg's cottage, the flight from invasion, and Griselda's death"

memes.label: "Edgar Jay-Blenkinsop"
memes.description: "A young antagonist at the anti-militarist meeting in Chapter XV, representing the complacent middle-class reformism that William comes to despise."

memes.label: "Bloomsbury"
memes.description: "A central London district where William Tully and Griselda made their first home in a tiny flat, and where Faraday's lodgings served as the birthplace of William's political awakening."

memes.label: "British Army"
memes.description: "The military institution that first rejected William Tully for his insufficient height, then later accepted him for clerical duties, representing both his thwarted desire for vengeance and the crushing monotony of bureaucratic war."

memes.label: "British Consul"
memes.description: "The British diplomatic representative in Brussels, invoked in Chapter VI as a symbol of pre-war faith in official channels and the established order of international relations."

memes.label: "Brussels"
memes.description: "The Belgian capital that William and Griselda reach during their flight southward — a city under imminent threat of German occupation, representing the collision of civilian life with military force."

memes.label: "Camberwell"
memes.description: "William's childhood home in south London — the lower-middle-class neighborhood that shaped his class identity and from which he spent his life trying to escape"

memes.label: "Cambridge Local Examination"
memes.description: "The Senior Cambridge Local Examination, William Tully's supreme academic achievement before entering the working world — an educational milestone that defines both his aspirations and his limits."

memes.label: "Camden Town"
memes.description: "A district of north London where William Tully took lodgings after the death of his wife Griselda, living in solitude and obsessive study of military history."

memes.label: "Imprint"
memes.description: "Publication imprint and copyright information"

memes.label: "Chapter I"
memes.description: "William Tully, a mild-mannered clerk, inherits money and meets Faraday"

memes.label: "Chapter II"
memes.description: "William and Faraday begin their friendship and social reform partnership"

memes.label: "Chapter III"
memes.description: "William and Faraday attend their first political meeting"

memes.description: "William and Faraday join the Social Reform movement"

memes.label: "Chapter V"
memes.description: "William attends a Fabian summer school"

memes.label: "Chapter VI"
memes.description: "William and Faraday at the summer school — debates and discussions"

memes.label: "Chapter VII"
memes.description: "William meets and falls in love with Edith Haynes"

memes.label: "Chapter VIII"
memes.description: "William and Edith's courtship and engagement"

memes.label: "Chapter IX"
memes.description: "William and Edith honeymoon in Belgium"

memes.label: "Chapter X"
memes.description: "The outbreak of World War I interrupts their holiday"

memes.label: "Chapter XI"
memes.description: "William and Edith return to England — war's impact on their lives"

memes.label: "Chapter XII"
memes.description: "William struggles with pacifist ideals vs. wartime pressure"

memes.label: "Chapter XIII"
memes.description: "Edith's changing attitudes toward the war"

memes.label: "Chapter XIV"
memes.description: "William faces increasing social isolation for his views"

memes.label: "Chapter XV"
memes.description: "Edith leaves William — the widening gulf"

memes.label: "Chapter XVI"
memes.description: "William's solitary resistance and reflection"

memes.description: "The war's final year and its toll"

memes.description: "William's fate — the aftermath"

memes.description: "Colophon and production notes"

memes.description: "Uncopyright notice — public domain dedication"

memes.label: "Charing Cross"
memes.description: "A central London railway terminus and district where William Tully arrived on his return from Belgium, determined to enlist, and where he later found intellectual solace at a secondhand bookstall."

memes.label: "Class"
memes.description: "The social stratification that defines William Tully's identity — from his lower-middle-class origins to his encounters with working-class solidarity and class-based prejudice"

memes.label: "Compulsory Service"
memes.description: "Mandatory military service — a policy William Tully opposed before the war on pacifist grounds while supporting compulsory trade unionism, exposing a contradiction at the heart of his political identity. Britain introduced conscription in 1916, drawing Faraday reluctantly into the army and to his death."

memes.label: Concept Index
memes.description: Complete index of all concept entries in William—An Englishman, sorted alphabetically by label.

memes.label: "Conscience"
memes.description: "The faculty of moral self-judgment that Hamilton presents as easily satisfied through rationalization — a comfortable companion rather than a demanding master"

memes.label: "Conscientious Objection"
memes.description: "The principled refusal of military service on grounds of conscience — a position taken by Faraday and initially by William Tully, but abandoned by both under the pressure of the First World War. The novel treats conscientious objection with unusual sympathy while dramatising its ultimate fragility against national crisis."

memes.label: "Courage"
memes.description: "The moral and physical courage that distinguishes characters like Griselda and the soldiers William encounters, contrasting with his own gradual development from timidity to resolve"

memes.label: "Democracy"
memes.description: "A core ideological keyword in William—An Englishman, invoked by the novel's progressive characters as both a political ideal and a rhetorical rallying-cry — and subjected to devastating irony by a narrative that depicts its fragility in the face of war."

memes.label: "Dieppe"
memes.description: "A French Channel port through which William Tully and Edith Haynes travelled on their return journey from occupied Belgium to England in August 1914."

memes.label: "Duty"
memes.description: "The moral framework that governs William's pre-war life — from his mother's 'duty' to her son to his own 'duty to the holiest of causes' — which the war transforms from abstract principle to concrete obligation"

memes.label: "Edith Haynes"
memes.description: "The capable Somerset landowner who befriends William Tully after the war, providing the novel's quiet counterpoint to Griselda — a woman shaped by war's losses rather than pre-war politics"

memes.label: "Education"
memes.description: "The theme of education in William—An Englishman: William Tully's journey from Board School scholarship through the Cambridge Local Examination to autodidact self-improvement at the Free Library, representing both aspiration and limitation in Edwardian working-class life."

memes.label: "Edwardes"
memes.description: "The Central London branch secretary of the Independent Socialist Party, a minor character at the anti-militarist meeting in Chapter XV who embodies the bureaucratic and committee-bound character of pre-war reformism."

memes.label: "The Enemy"
memes.description: "The construction of the enemy in wartime — from William's early heckling of 'enemy speakers' to the reality of enemy occupation in Belgium — and how the concept transforms his moral universe"

memes.label: "England"
memes.description: "The nation William Tully belongs to by birth and loses by experience — the England of platform certainties that becomes, after Belgium, a foreign country whose language of patriotism, duty, and recruitment he can no longer speak."

memes.label: "European War"
memes.description: "The period term used throughout the novel for the First World War (1914–1918), reflecting the contemporary vocabulary of 1919 when the book was published."

memes.label: Event Index
memes.description: Complete index of all event entries in William—An Englishman, sorted alphabetically by label.

memes.label: "Faith"
memes.description: "The capacity for absolute belief that William Tully loses through his wartime experiences — his 'faith in his prewar creed' — marking his transition from ideological certainty to complex doubt"

memes.label: "Faraday"
memes.description: "William's fellow-clerk and intellectual mentor who introduces him to social reform, anti-militarism, and the world of progressive politics"

memes.label: "Fear"
memes.description: "The primal emotion that Hamilton traces from abstract pre-war anxiety through the mortal terror of the battlefield-adjacent civilian, arriving at a psychologically nuanced portrait of fear as the war's most pervasive wound."

memes.label: "Folkestone"
memes.description: "An English Channel port where William Tully arrived from Dieppe on his return from occupied Belgium, and where he first resolved to become a soldier."

memes.label: Forest of Arden
memes.description: The forested region in the Belgian Ardennes where William Tully and Griselda spent their honeymoon, and where they first encountered the outbreak of the First World War.

memes.label: "France"
memes.description: "The primary theater of the Western Front where William serves as a soldier, and the nation whose revolutionary tradition contrasts with English gradualism"

memes.label: "Free Library"
memes.description: "The public lending library that serves as William Tully's site of self-education and emotional recovery after his bereavement, representing the autodidact tradition central to the novel's portrait of working-class intellectual life."

memes.label: "Freedom"
memes.description: "The ideal of freedom that motivates both the suffrage movement and William's rejection of his clerkdom, evolving from an abstract ideal to a concrete experience of constraint and loss"

memes.label: Gare du Nord
memes.description: The Paris railway terminus where William Tully first arrived in the French capital after his flight from the Belgian Ardennes, marking his transition from the war zone back to civilisation.

memes.label: "Germany"
memes.description: "The enemy nation — represented in the novel by soldiers like Heinz and by the abstract threat of militarism that destroys William's Belgian idyll"

memes.label: Great Civil War
memes.description: The militant suffrage movement's term for its own struggle, used by Griselda to describe the fight for women's votes — a metaphor that collapses when confronted with actual warfare.

memes.label: "Grief"
memes.description: "The overwhelming emotion of loss that punctuates William Tully's life — from his mother's death to the shattering loss of Griselda — and drives his psychological transformation"

memes.label: "Griselda Watkins / Tully"
memes.description: "The female protagonist of Cicely Hamilton's William—An Englishman (1919): a young militant suffragette whose courtship with William Tully culminates in a July 1914 wedding and a Belgian honeymoon shattered by the German invasion. Hamilton portrays her as the fiercer, more ideological partner—a pacifist whose certainties the war tests more violently than her husband's, and whose courage in the refugee flight reveals reservoirs of resilience beneath the platform rhetoric."

memes.label: Harrow Road
memes.description: A London thoroughfare where the German soldier Heinz worked as a hairdresser before the war, serving as an ironic point of connection between England and its enemy.

memes.label: "Hatred"
memes.description: "The destructive but transformative emotion that catalyzes William Tully's political awakening, from his hatred of Jay-Blenkinsop to the smouldering hatred of wartime"

memes.label: "Heinz"
memes.description: "A German soldier who speaks English from working at a Harrow Road hairdresser — his interaction with William and Griselda humanizes the enemy and complicates the war's moral binaries"

memes.label: "Home"
memes.description: "The concept of home as domestic sanctuary, site of political identity, and object of wartime longing in William—An Englishman. From the Bloomsbury flat to the Belgian cottage, each domestic setting marks a stage in William Tully's political and personal development."

memes.label: "Horse Guards Parade"
memes.description: "A landmark in Whitehall, central London, where a military recruiting tent was stationed during the First World War — a site of symbolic significance in the novel's wartime London geography."

memes.label: "Humanity"
memes.description: "The concept of a common humanity — tested and transformed by war — in William—An Englishman."

memes.label: "Internationalism"
memes.description: "The pre-1914 conviction, shared by William Tully and Faraday, that cross-border working-class solidarity made international warfare impossible — a creed the novel treats as a form of political faith rather than knowledge, and which the outbreak of the Great War decisively shattered."

memes.label: "Jay-Blenkinsop"
memes.description: "A figure of complacent civilian authority who becomes the target of William's wartime hatred — representing everything William has lost: safety, certainty, moral simplicity"

memes.label: "Kaiser Wilhelm II"
memes.description: "The German Emperor during World War I, referenced throughout the novel as a symbol of Prussian militarism and the enemy against whom Britain fights."

memes.label: "Lord Kitchener"
memes.description: "Horatio Herbert Kitchener, British field marshal and Secretary of State for War, whose 1914 call for volunteers and prophecy of a three-year war looms over the novel's wartime narrative."

memes.label: "Labour Party"
memes.description: "The Labour Party of Edwardian Britain -- the parliamentary voice of the working class that radicals like Faraday denounce as treacherous compromisers, functioning in the novel as the 'establishment left' against which more militant figures define their revolutionary credentials."

memes.label: "London"
memes.description: "The primary setting for the novel's first half — Edwardian London where William works as an insurance clerk, attends political meetings, and meets Griselda. Hamilton renders the city as a geography of class, politics, and alienation."

memes.label: "Madame Amberg"
memes.description: "A cosmopolitan female revolutionist, possibly of Russian origin, whose Belgian cottage becomes the novel's political center and the site of William and Griselda's fateful honeymoon"

memes.label: "Madame Amberg's Pamphlets"
memes.description: "The revolutionary pamphlets written in multiple languages by Madame Amberg, a cosmopolitan agitator whose suppressed publications represent the transnational radical press of the pre-First World War era."

memes.label: "Madame Peys"
memes.description: "A Belgian farm-housekeeper whose presence at Madame Amberg's cottage provides comic relief through the language barrier between Flemish and English"

memes.label: "Marriage"
memes.description: "The institution of marriage as experienced and transformed by William Tully and Griselda Watkins, from a progressive 'companionate' ideal to a bond tested by war and loss."

memes.label: "Memory"
memes.description: "The faculty through which the past persists in the present — from William's 'first distinct memory' to the evolving 'memory of Griselda' that shapes his post-war identity"

memes.label: "Militarism"
memes.description: "The ideology of military values and preparedness that William Tully converts to from his earlier pacifism, catalyzed by his wartime experiences in Belgium"

memes.label: Most Referenced
memes.description: Statistical cross-section of the William—An Englishman wiki showing the most-cited pages within each major category. Uses dynamic query blocks sorted by total citations.

memes.label: "Joachim Murat"
memes.description: "Napoleon's marshal, referenced in the novel alongside Ney and Augereau as an emblem of the individual military glory impossible in modern industrialized warfare"

memes.label: "Napoleon Bonaparte"
memes.description: "Referenced in the novel as a historical contrast — the great military figure whose individualism and ambition are impossible in the modern, industrialized warfare William experiences"

memes.label: "Nationalism"
memes.description: "The ideology of national identity and allegiance that William Tully reluctantly embraces after his traumatic experiences in the Belgian war zone, replacing his previous commitment to international socialism."

memes.label: "New Idea"
memes.description: "A recurring motif in William—An Englishman, the 'New Idea' represents progressive, socialist, and feminist thought that challenges established Victorian/Edwardian convictions — and the fear it provokes in those committed to the old order."

memes.label: "New York"
memes.description: "One of the global cities referenced in William—An Englishman as part of the cosmopolitan backdrop against which the novel's characters move, representing the interconnected world shattered by the European War."

memes.label: "Order"
memes.description: "The concept of social and political order — its disruption and defence — in William—An Englishman."

memes.label: Organization Index
memes.description: Complete index of all organization entries in William—An Englishman, sorted alphabetically by label.

memes.label: "Outbreak of War"
memes.description: "The eruption of the First World War in August 1914 — the historical catastrophe that shatters the novel's progressive world and initiates William Tully's transformation from platform socialist to traumatised survivor."

memes.label: "Pacifism"
memes.description: "The anti-war conviction that challenges and ultimately fails the novel's socialist characters when confronted with the reality of the Great War"

memes.label: "Paris"
memes.description: "Referenced as the cosmopolitan alternative to London — a city associated with revolutionary exile, artistic freedom, and the internationalism the war destroys"

memes.label: "Patriotism"
memes.description: "The nascent national feeling that William Tully discovers through his wartime experiences, marking his transformation from abstract cosmopolitan to engaged Englishman"

memes.label: "Peace"
memes.description: "The concept of peace — personal, political, and international — as it appears in William—An Englishman, contrasted with the outbreak of the First World War."

memes.label: Person Index
memes.description: Complete index of all person entries in William—An Englishman, sorted alphabetically by label.

memes.label: "Philippe"
memes.description: "A young farm-boy at Madame Amberg's Belgian cottage who represents the rural innocence soon to be destroyed by war"

memes.label: Place Index
memes.description: Complete index of all place entries in William—An Englishman, sorted alphabetically by label.

memes.label: "Power"
memes.description: "The nature and exercise of power — political, military, and personal — as explored in William—An Englishman."

memes.label: "Progress"
memes.description: "The Edwardian faith in gradual social improvement that William Tully inherits from Faraday and the Fabian milieu, which the war exposes as a comforting illusion"

memes.label: "Recruiting Campaign"
memes.description: "The British Army recruitment drive for Kitchener's Army during World War I, referenced in the novel as a force that transforms William Tully's relationship to war and nation."

memes.label: Red Cross
memes.description: The humanitarian organisation whose ambulances and hospital trains appear in the novel's wartime scenes, representing medical relief amid the destruction of the First World War.

memes.label: "Religion"
memes.description: "Religious faith as a residual presence in the novel, appearing most often in moments of extremity when political and rational frameworks fail the characters."

memes.label: "Revolution"
memes.description: "The international revolutionary politics embodied by Madame Amberg, representing the most extreme form of political commitment in the novel"

memes.label: "Rue Ernest Dupont"
memes.description: "A Brussels street where a wartime administrative office is located in the novel, serving as a setting for William Tully's post-combat desk work and his processing of the war's meaning."

memes.label: "Russia"
memes.description: "Referenced through Madame Amberg's putative Russian origin and the broader context of revolutionary internationalism that haunts the novel's political imagination"

memes.label: "Social Reform"
memes.description: "The Edwardian belief, held fervently by William and Griselda, that Society could be improved through the well-meaning efforts of well-meaning souls — a faith the novel treats with sustained irony."

memes.label: "Socialism"
memes.description: "The political faith that shapes William Tully's intellectual awakening and later dissolves under the pressure of war — pre-war socialism as Hamilton presents it: earnest, combative, and fundamentally unequipped for the reality it encounters"

memes.label: "The State"
memes.description: "The abstract entity of governmental power that pre-war socialist William Tully sought to transform through reform — and whose military apparatus he later attempted to join as a recruit."

memes.label: "Suffrage"
memes.description: "The militant women's suffrage movement that Griselda Haynes belongs to, representing active political engagement in contrast to William's abstract reformism"

memes.label: "Suffragette Movement"
memes.description: "The militant women's suffrage movement -- the WSPU led by Emmeline and Christabel Pankhurst -- which formed Griselda Watkins's political identity, gave her the courage and tactical training that sustained her through the Belgian crisis, and represents in the novel both the transformative power and the contextual limitations of political conviction."

memes.label: "The Arrest"
memes.description: "The arrests of suffragette militants, including Griselda, which test political conviction against state power and physical suffering"

memes.label: "The Cause"
memes.description: "\"The Cause\" — the suffrage movement as invoked by William Tully and Griselda throughout William—An Englishman, representing the shared political faith that binds them together before the war destroys the world in which such faith was possible."

memes.label: "The Cottage"
memes.description: "Madame Amberg's cottage in the heart of the Belgian Ardennes — the honeymoon idyll that becomes a trap, the last space of safety before the German invasion, and the site where pre-war innocence transforms into mortal fear."

memes.label: "The Enlistment"
memes.description: "William Tully's decision to enlist in the British Army upon returning to London — the culmination of his transformation from anti-militarist activist to volunteer soldier, and his repeated rejection on grounds of insufficient height."

memes.label: "The Escape"
memes.description: "The escapes — from arrest, from danger, from Belgium — that punctuate the novel's wartime chapters and reveal character under extreme pressure"

memes.label: "The Flight from Belgium"
memes.description: "William and Griselda's desperate flight through the Belgian countryside as the German army advances, testing their courage and their bond"

memes.label: "The Funeral"
memes.description: "The improvised burial of Griselda in a Belgian village cemetery — a makeshift funeral without coffin, mourners, or ceremony, conducted under the shadow of the German advance, marking the novel's definitive rupture between the old world of social ritual and the new reality of war"

memes.label: "The Honeymoon"
memes.description: "William and Griselda Tully's walking tour through the Belgian Ardennes in late July 1914 — the novel's central pastoral interlude, cut short by the German invasion that transforms their romantic idyll into a flight for survival."

memes.label: "The Insurance Office"
memes.description: "The London insurance office where William Tully works as a clerk — the site of his pre-war employment, his friendship with Faraday, and the narrow world of lower-middle-class respectability from which political radicalism and war will deliver him."

memes.label: "The Meeting"
memes.description: "The political meeting culture of Edwardian Britain as a defining social institution — not a single event but the world of platforms, stewards, pamphlets, and oratory that shaped William Tully's identity, forged his partnership with Griselda, and served as Hamilton's satirical lens on pre-war progressive politics."

memes.label: "The Outbreak of War"
memes.description: "The outbreak of the First World War in August 1914 as experienced by William and Griselda Tully during their honeymoon in the Belgian Ardennes — the historical rupture that transforms the novel from a story of political idealism into a story of survival."

memes.label: "The Protest"
memes.description: "The suffragette protests — window-smashing, public confrontations, arrests — that shape Griselda's character and represent political commitment as embodied action rather than abstract belief"

memes.label: "The Refugee Crisis"
memes.description: "The displacement of Belgian civilians by the German invasion, witnessed by William and Griselda as they flee through the countryside"

memes.label: "The Shooting of the Hostages"
memes.description: "The summary execution of two Belgian civilians by German soldiers in August 1914, witnessed by William and Griselda Tully — the pivotal event that shatters William's anti-militarist faith and converts him to a nascent patriotism."

memes.label: "*The Torch*"
memes.description: "A radical socialist periodical edited by Faraday, serving as the mouthpiece of advanced political opinion and the vehicle for William Tully's political education before the First World War."

memes.label: "The Tract on the Woman Question"
memes.description: "William Tully's unfinished polemical tract on the Woman Question — the writing project he begins during his Belgian honeymoon, representing his intellectual ambition and brief transformation from clerk to 'Social Reformer' before the war interrupts."

memes.label: "The Wedding"
memes.description: "William Tully and Griselda Watkins's wedding on July 23rd, 1914 — a self-consciously progressive ceremony that omitted the obedience vow, marking their union as a political statement as much as a personal commitment."

memes.label: "The Woman Question"
memes.description: "The Woman Question — the Edwardian debate about women's roles, rights, and emancipation that forms the intellectual backdrop to William—An Englishman. Distinct from William Tully's unfinished tract on the subject, this concept page surveys the historical discourse within which the novel's characters thought and argued."

memes.label: "Trade Unionism"
memes.description: "The organised labour movement — a compulsory solidarity William Tully embraced while opposing compulsory military service, exposing a contradiction Cicely Hamilton uses to satirise the selective ethics of Edwardian sectarian politics."

memes.label: "Vindex"
memes.description: "The pseudonym under which William Tully writes for The Torch, a reformist periodical. 'Vindex' represents William's pre-war identity as a confident political commentator."

memes.label: "Votes for Women"
memes.description: "The specific political demand of the suffrage movement — votes for women — which William 'of course' supports as an abstract principle without fully grasping its radical implications"

memes.label: "War"
memes.description: "The pervasive armed conflict that transforms William Tully and Griselda from idealistic socialists into refugees, satirising the machinery of nationalism and propaganda in Cicely Hamilton's 1919 novel."

memes.label: "Whitehall"
memes.description: "The central London thoroughfare housing government ministries, where a military recruiting tent stood during the First World War — a symbolic locus of state power and the war effort in the novel."

memes.label: "William—An Englishman"
memes.description: "Cicely Hamilton's 1919 First World War novel following William Tully from socialist platform orator to Belgian refugee, winner of the Prix Femina-Vie Heureuse — a satirical and psychologically acute study of how war destroys the certainties of progressive thought."

memes.label: "William Tully"
memes.description: "The protagonist of Cicely Hamilton's William—An Englishman (1919): a young London clerk and socialist whose Belgian honeymoon is shattered by the German invasion, transforming him from an earnest platform orator into a refugee and, ultimately, an alien in his own country."

memes.label: "Wright"
memes.description: "A British soldier and D.C.M. recipient whose severe shell-shock renders him a figure of contagious terror during the air raid in Chapter XVIII of William—An Englishman."
